speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number 3. Today's date is March 27, 1996. We are conducting an interview with the survivor Mr. Marvin Zborowski. Mr. Zborowski, you were describing the bunker, the hiding place in the attic. Would you please continue tell us about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I stated, in that house, we were upstairs, four of us. My mother-- may she rest in peace, my brother-- older one, myself, and my sister, the youngest. Four people were there. The condition in-- in that place was bearable. In a way, there was no heat, but we were covered. Food-- we were brought up by-- by Mr. Placzek. It was sufficient, but it was very difficult in as far the hygienics are concerned.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when you had your physical needs, was it all upstairs? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no canalization or any means. Therefore, it's interesting to state that the human body-- whatever it's consuming, it's giving practically back the same amount. In other words, we received one pail of water, and another pail was used just for the disposals.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many times a day did he come to pick up the bucket?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was only done at nighttime. And I must say, quite a big devotion for a man to do that. Everyday, he took down one pail of disposals. He made us very comfortable. In other words, to be able to sit down on the pail, he even created a sitting board out of wood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me-- I'd like you to describe a day upstairs. And tell us whether you were sitting, or lying down, and what you were eating, and what were you talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother was telling us family stories, which, really, it's not the time and place to repeat, going back from fathers and forefathers about the heritage of our people. Besides, being that Eli was three years older, he was somehow, some way, giving me some kind of lessons or reading books-- of which I did not mention before, the town was quite culturally well established. We had a library with cases of books which were never used, and they were burned by the Germans. But the Gentile family that we were in hiding collected a lot of books, which we, in turn, used them during our stay in the bunker.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he feed you during the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- mainly, the main staple food was bread and potatoes. And he was limited also of even buying it, because his family consisted just of one daughter. Therefore, he cannot go and buy, let's say, 5 pounds of bread when he had only one person with him, so to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he used to buy it in a different bakery. In other words, he went to one bakery. And then he went in the afternoon to another bakery, and he bought it. And how many times did he come up to the attic?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say that he come up, most of-- I would say once, and especially to comfort my mother. Because in the first months when we were in the hiding, Father was still between the 30 chosen Jews to work. And as a matter of fact, in that period of time, that in the first months of our hiding in town, he came to visit us. It was a holiday, national or a religious holiday, a Christian holiday, and he was off for it. So he came at night and left at night. He could not be seen, that he walks into the house where we are in hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he look to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He looked quite, I would say, swollen. The job that he had-- in the last month, they had nothing what to do. Those 30 Jews practically were just practically hostages to the Germans. They were just pressing for money. They kept them just superficially that they are needed. They were not needed at all. They should have been simply just sent away the same way as all the others. But being that they were 30 rich Jews, and they were pressing constantly for money-- they gave them constantly money. And they extended their stay for another month, another week.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did they do? They-- they were building a stable for horses. Whatever they built at daytime, they destroyed it at night, the Germans themselves-- to be able to build. So how long could you build a stable? So my father was working at the section that where they were making the mortar. And this is a job outside. And probably, he got kidney affected, or kidney sickness, so he got swollen. So they used to call him [GERMAN], that means to say that, 'The [? Full--' ?] how could I say even, this expression? [? 'Full-eaten'-- ?] I mean, 'Obese.' He looked obese. He looked fat.

speaker: interviewee
text: 'The Full, Obese Man,' but in an 'orogatory' statement. I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: 'Derogatory.'

speaker: interviewee
text: --I don't think that it being used here. But in [INAUDIBLE] it's hard to say. But anyhow, so he looked sick. But he helped. He could have stayed with us, but he was afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the last time you saw your--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the last time I did see my father. That was in 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you recall from that meeting?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was so good. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: [SOBBING] So protective.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Practically every child at that young age had some coins, some diamonds, some kind of possession. And my mother used to cry, 'Why do you-- why you divide your possessions between those small children? What do you want?'

speaker: interviewee
text: But he said, 'I don't know where they're going to be tomorrow. Or how we're going to be split up, and how we're going to be divided in different places, in different--' and so on. So therefore-- I mean, he was very bright. But he knew that it's no-- it's nothing [? great, ?] but there was no way of escaping. There was no way of getting any help. There was no way that he could have done more that he did. And if-- and if-- we survived, and I would say definitely by the grace of God, as I am a believer. And of-- of the grace of my father, that he protected his wife and children. But he could not protect himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after he left you, he went back to the-- to the place where he stayed?

speaker: interviewee
text: He went back. Then one day, where practically the Germans cleaned out all the towns where they called it 'judenrein,' meaning--

speaker: interviewer
text: 'Free of Jews.'

speaker: interviewee
text: --'free of Jews.' So the head of the police, or the Gestapo, so to say-- 'You cannot-- I cannot keep you as-- I kept you. Look, my dear people.' And it was true. 'I kept you as long as I can. I have to send you away. I mean, I cannot keep you.'

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were actually-- everyday, they were counted. They had the, so to say, the-- 'Appell.'

speaker: interviewee
text: --the 'appell,' so to say, they get together. They were counted each day-- if there's 30, if it's not 31, if it's only 29, and so on. These countings were performed in the synagogue, the only synagogue that survived. And it stays until this day. At one day, they said, 'This is it.' And they locked them up and said, 'No way out. You stay here, right here. That's it.' They stayed there for three days. On the third day, then they were really afraid that they're going to be eventually shot, because there was no way that they should put them somewhere for to work or whatever. Anyhow, at the last day, or so to say, the last hours before they took those 30 people out, my father and two other young men escaped through a window, through the back window of the synagogue. They jumped the synagogue. And we, being in town, heard the Poles were stating that three Jews run away from those 30. And between the three is Zborowski, that they-- they glorified. That name was, by them, a glory, so to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we, in turn, were happy that he ran away. So the two young men run, let's say, in one direction of one street outside of the town, and my father in the other. The other two were caught by the Poles. They were brought back to the synagogue.

speaker: interviewee
text: They actually should have been shot. But the German leader there, knowing that he took from them so much possession, let them live, although they committed, so to say, a crime by running away. And my father seemingly succeeded running away. And he was killed by the Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: They caught him.

speaker: interviewee
text: They got them. They killed him.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you learn of the death of your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did not learn until this day. He never returned. If he would have survived that first day, so to say, he would have come up to the-- to our hiding place.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when did you hear about his murder?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were a lot of bodies that they found that-- of Jews being killed by-- by Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were now in hiding in Mr. Placzek. I'd like to take you back. You described the attic, but you said that Mr. Placzek also built for you an emergency bunker. Will you please describe the second hiding place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the reason-- the purpose for the second hiding place was that the town, as I stated before, was on the German border and in a crossing point. Many times, this city was visited by-- by outside Germans controlling even the Poles. So when that kind of rations, or that kind of checkings came into town, we had to go down to the cellar. In the cellar, he built a double wall, which was really a strong hiding bunker place. It was really ingenuity to establish that kind of thinking. He cut off a piece of the cellar and put-- physically built, with mortar and bricks, a wall which was movable on wooden wheels underneath. And in there, to be able-- so we move this, and we can slide in there in the back of that part.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you are able to sit? Or did you only could-- were you only standing?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- we were able to sit, but there was no air. If we lit up a match, the match did not burn. It was no oxygen. It was very little oxygen.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how long a time could you have possibly stayed in that hiding place?

speaker: interviewee
text: We don't know exactly, because we never stayed that long. But it was quiet, and it was with shelves made. In other words, when we went in, we could put up the shelves, wooden shelves, and put something on the shelves, and put-- and move the wall. We could do ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have occasion to go there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we had quite a few occasion. I would not say that many times, so there were quite a few occasions that we had to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe, how did it feel emotionally to have to run from one hiding place to another place, and not knowing who is upstairs, what's happening? Can you recount to us any of your emotions or feelings?

speaker: interviewee
text: The emotions or feelings was-- I was too young really to realize. I was even upset about it. I would like to scream and be rebellious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Being at-- at the attic, one day, I do remember, I was-- the roof had the-- the [? [GERMAN]. ?] How do you say? The slates?

speaker: interviewee
text: The slates on the roof. So I moved one of them, and I tried to look out to the world, so to say. And I did see my bicycle. A friend of mine was riding my bicycle. It's my bicycle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why couldn't I ride that bicycle? Where is-- why? What did I do wrong? That's the emotion that I had. And also, what could I think? I did not wear any shoes. We were just wearing just rags around the feet, because it was cold. We washed ourselves. Still and all, we were not clean.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had a problem. Sister got once an infection and red stripe on her arm. We could see that she may lose an arm. And there was no medication. So we-- we had to go out, and we had to bring some-- some-- medication for her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how did that happen? Who brought the medication?

speaker: interviewee
text: He bought it. And I think that once, also, my brother went and bought some medication in a drugstore. But I think the Gentile did it-- I mean, the man, Mr. Placzek did. He brought some medication. Also, one of the incident that I do remember, that we were always given food at a given hour. At one time, I was just-- the house, like I stated, was not finished. So I walked over to the corner of the, so to say, of the attic, and looking down if Mr. or Mrs. Placzek put the food already down next to the ladder. While I'm looking down, a little boy stares just straight into my eyes. And he starts to scream. He says, 'Mrs. Placzek!'

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Placzek is upstairs. So she says, 'Yes? Oh, that's wonderful.' Then all of a sudden, this Mr. Placzek comes in. He comes home through the door. So the little boy said, 'How did you fly down from the roof?' So I can tell you one thing-- we really were very panicky after that little boy. And we were thinking, if he gets home and goes home, what kind of story he will make up, and what will he do, and what will he say? So we constantly lived in fear. We always were worried about what the next hour, not the next day, will bring.

speaker: interviewee
text: Also, one of the most important thing is that we had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the situation of-- of that my brother went out one day. We were in line of communications with relatives that were in a village, also in hiding. And they wrote to us if we could help give money for a cousin which was given away, and also by a Gentile family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day, my brother disguised himself as a lady, because he was known as a boy. And he dressed himself up as a girl. He had blond hair. He had no big beard, so to say. He was young, too-- he was three years older.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he walked out in the market day barefoot, because there was no shoes that-- ladies' shoes could fit his legs. And he was walking barefoot in the market, going into that house and telling the people that he was sent by this and this family, by his uncle and aunt-- and not even-- he did not say 'uncle,' but sent by this man and to give money for the child, which they prepaid, and so on. So that's the time when really it started. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned before, when your sister had the infection, that Mr. Placzek went to get the medication. And you mentioned something in passing, that Eli also went. Did Eli, again, walk out-- out of the hiding to go to a drugstore to pick up medication?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- if I recall, Eli left the hiding place a few times, which normally, shouldn't have been done. One time, I think he went out because of the sickness of my sister to the drugstore. And one time, he went also to-- to change-- to get a gold coin, to sell some gold for currency. And this was the two times that I do recall that he went out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he disguise himself both times, or did he walk out as Eli?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he did not as-- he was disguised.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was disguised. Now, how long do you stay in Mr. Placzek's house?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed in Mr. Placzek house for 18 months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. Why did you leave it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The problem-- I started to tell you. When we got this letter from the relatives to help, and Eli decided to go to help, we were a very close-knitted family. Until this day, we are very close. He went to this Gentile to pay for the child, because my uncle prepaid only for, let's say, for one year. And now, it was more, so he prepaid. And then he asked also if he could prepay, if they have another place for another child.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he did not say who he is. But the Gentile sent a little boy after my brother, meaning after he was dressed as a girl. He was wearing special costumes like the peasants, and stripes, and with little [? [POLISH]. ?] Yeah, the Polish--

speaker: interviewee
text: --Polish costume for the market day.

speaker: interviewer
text: --national costume.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he went. And he was sending a little boy, and he did not know that he's being spied.

speaker: interviewer
text: Being followed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they followed him. And he went into the house of hiding. Now, there were rumors. Then a week later, he came back. And there were starting to be rumors that Mr. Placzek has to do something with Jews. They couldn't dream or think about that Mr. Placzek, who is a devoted Pole, and even being considered as a [? Volksdeutsche, ?] which means to say that he's partially German-- that he is-- that he has to do anything with Jews. Regardless, when he went the next day, for-- for whatever reason, Eli identified himself that he is Eli Zborowski. At that time, it means to say that a week before, when he went into Placzek, that Placzek has to do with the Zborowskis. And they probably may be even there. They start to denounce Placzek to the local authorities. But he was so friendly with the local authorities, entertaining them. While we were in hiding upstairs, they were entertained for our money downstairs. And where we were-- when they were downstairs, he-- whenever he had company in the house, he put in a special bulb, a light that we should behave and be quiet. Until this day, you will never hear me sneezing, because I always-- when I was in hiding, I had to take a little pillow and killed the sneeze and choked my sneezing, not to be heard downstairs or outside at the sidewalk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, so--

speaker: interviewee
text: So when he came back-- during the second time when he went, he did not-- knowing that he's being followed, he did not come back to our hiding place, and he went to the village where the other part of our relatives stayed there. After a while, rumors were spreading unbelievable about the Placzek family. So when one day, Eli came back to-- to the bunker, we decided that we have to go out. In the beginning, Mrs. Placzek and Mr. Placzek was absolutely against that we should leave. And the reason was quite simple. They were reasoning with us. They said, 'Look, if you're going to be caught, the children will-- they will definitely say where they were hiding.' And she was really afraid that we will denounce her. Not because we wanted to denounce-- because we were just children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one night, we decided to move, and this was in the summer months. The night is short. The days are long. We could not start to move out of the hiding until it's really-- Nighttime.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Late. Beside, it is a curfew. And the people were sitting in the backyards. We had to wait almost until midnight. At midnight, we starting to move out. We happened to be-- as small as this little town was, we were in the western part of the city. We had to go east. West was the border. We were hot on the border. It's a border. It's police. So we decided to go. The men--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you going?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were decided that we will go to the hiding place, to that village where my other relatives are. So going there, we did not know exactly how to go. Mr. Placzek, the man who was hiding us, dressed up himself in his honorary uniform. He was from the honorary [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] fire department, and he put out his uniform. And so to say, that he is-- in case if he's being checked or being asked any questions, that he could identify himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he took us around that city to cross the river. And he said he would take us all the way. We kept on saying, 'It's not needed. It's not necessary.' But he kept on insisting that he wants to see us on the other side of the town.

speaker: interviewee
text: While we reached that river-- that river that I remember I used to play always, jumping back and forth-- that river, it was a rain probably a few days before, a week before. That river was quite deep. And it became a little bit wider, as I remember. Everybody could cross it. My mother, may she rest in peace, couldn't do it. I was wearing-- it was summer months, but I was wearing high boots.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did I wear boots? It's all so silly. I had, in the boots, built in some golden coins. So I did not want to give up the golden coins.

speaker: interviewee
text: The boots were small. The boots were made two years ago. So I grew in the two years, if not that much. The boots, I can hardly fit in, bu I got in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally, when I had to cross that river, and I had to carry my mother, I said, 'Look, I have boots. I would not even barely touch the water. Why don't you let me carry you?'

speaker: interviewee
text: While I was carrying Mother over the water, I went into the water. The water penetrated inside to my foot. I cannot walk. I had water in my boots. I cannot walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm crossing. The time was running out on us, because 4 or 5 o'clock in the morning, the day breaks again. So my brother took off-- from one leg, he took off my boot. Maybe took me a half an hour more even to take off one. He said, 'I'm not going to start to take off this one.' So I said, 'You know what? Let's walk.' I started to walk with the water in that foot, and I starting to walk. We passed by the brick factory. We were already on the-- on the east side of the town.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Mr. Placzek, the honorary chief of the fire department, says goodbye to us, crying, saying goodbye and good luck. I felt pains in my leg. We're walking a little bit. And I fell down, and couldn't walk. Whole leg got stiff. And I begged my family, and I said, 'Look, let me die here. Just continue. There's no way that I can go on. I am just simply paralyzed, so to say, with one leg. Let me die here. Continue.' My brother said, and my mother said, 'Nothing doing. We will definitely not leave you here. But we cannot go on like this.'

speaker: interviewer
text: We decided-- day was starting to break. My brother decided, and he said, 'You know what? I know-- I was there a few weeks ago. I was in that village. I know exactly how to go to that village.

speaker: interviewee
text: Let me go by myself into that village. I will try to-- to hire a horse and a buggy. Maybe the man-- the other man has a horse and a buggy, and I will pick-- pick you up and put you in a wagon. You'll be able to walk.'

speaker: interviewer
text: A good idea. We looked at the time. I said, 'Eli, it's 5 o'clock. When will you-- how long will it take you?'

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, 'Look, it will take me maybe an hour to go one way, an hour the other way. Figure an hour to convince the guy. By 12 o'clock, definitely, I will be back.' 12 o'clock passed by. Let me just go back one second-- while we were there, we didn't have anything what to eat. So-- so we had a bottle with honey, but the bottle was dry. The honey dries.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we used to take a stick from a pine tree and dipped it in, and Mother gave each child just to suck a little bit of that honey. There was no food. Not that we were hungry. Just-- it's a mother's instinct to give the children something. She didn't have anything else to give, so she gave just to suck this. When Eli went back to the village, and you left-- you were left with your mother and your sister, were you hiding in the woods? Were you on the riverbank? Describe, where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were already away from the river, deep inside the inland already, inside to the east. And we were hiding in a ditch.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a ditch.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he covered us with branches. And he said that he knows exactly where he's dropping us off. So when it was 12 o'clock, 1 o'clock, 2 o'clock, 4 o'clock, 5 o'clock in the evening, we do not see Eli. And we watched how the sun goes down. How the local people, they're taking the cows in already, and they're passing by. They don't see us, thank God. And we don't see Eli. Around maybe 6 o'clock or later-- the sun was really coming down-- Eli found us. He said, 'Look, I'm looking for you already for two hours. I couldn't find you where you are.'

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he bring with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Instead of bringing a horse-- so the horse that he thought that he is going to get, the horse was dying. And he couldn't get another horse. So he said, 'You know what? We will start to walk.'

speaker: interviewer
text: So during the time that he was not here, I started to be able to move my leg a little bit. It-- I could bend, let's put it this way. 'If I bend,' I said, 'maybe I will be able to walk.'

speaker: interviewee
text: When he starting-- when he came back, he came back with the Gentile, Mr. Kolacz's sister. She's supposed to help. But she was so panicky, so she said, and she ran back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we starting to walk. Eli did not like it, because we passed by people, and they were seeing that this is Jews. This is not walking of our peasant people. We know how to walk. This is not the walking.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, so Eli decided to do it a different way. He said, 'Let me start-- I will take Mother and Sister first. And then I will come back for you. I will carry you.'
